full_response:
Algorithm (doubling, “out-and-back’’)  

Let step ← 1            // length of the next probe  
Let dir  ← +1            // +1 = to the right, –1 = to the left  

while the crumb has not been reached do  
 1. walk step·dir inches from the origin;            // exploratory walk  
  if the crumb is encountered on the way, stop and succeed  
 2. walk step·(–dir) inches to return to the origin; // come back  
 3. step ← 2·step ;                                  // double search radius  
 4. dir  ← –dir ;                                    // change direction  
end while  

(The ant always starts the next probe from the origin and each successive
probe is twice as long as the previous one, while alternating directions.)

Correctness  

After the i-th probe the ant has examined every point whose distance
from the origin is at most 2^{i} inches.  
Because the probing distance doubles every iteration, sooner or later the
distance  |d| of the crumb will be ≤ the length of a probe; at that moment
the ant walks past the crumb and detects it.  Hence the algorithm is
correct.

Competitive-ratio analysis  

Let d = |crumb position| and assume, without loss of generality,
that the crumb is to the right of the origin (the analysis for the left
side is identical).  
Pick the unique integer k such that
      2^{k} < d ≤ 2^{k+1}.                                     (1)

The direction sequence of successive probes is
      R(1), L(2), R(4), L(8), … ;          // R = right, L = left

Thus

• probe 0 walks 1 inch to the right and back;  
• probe 1 walks 2 inches to the left and back;  
  …  
• probe k walks 2^{k} inches to the right and back;  
• probe k+1 walks 2^{k+1} inches to the left and back;  
• probe k+2 starts to the right for 2^{k+2} inches, and the crumb,
  whose distance d satisfies (1), is encountered during this
  (k+2)-nd exploratory walk.

Total distance already travelled before the final (k+2)-nd walk:

        Σ_{i=0}^{k+1} 2·2^{i}  =  2(2^{k+2} – 1)  =  2^{k+3} – 2.        (2)

Distance travelled on the final walk until the crumb is reached:  d.

Hence the whole tour length L satisfies

        L  ≤  (2^{k+3} – 2) + d
           <  2^{k+3} + d.                                           (3)

Because of (1) we have 2^{k} < d, so 2^{k+3} = 8·2^{k} < 8d.
Substituting in (3),

        L  <  8d + d  =  9d.

Since the optimal (omniscient) trajectory is just d inches,
the competitive ratio of the algorithm is strictly less than 9,
i.e. at most 9.  Therefore the algorithm is 9-competitive.

Complexities  

• Time (total inches walked): at most 9|d|, i.e. O(|d|).  
• Space: O(1) (only step and dir are stored).  

Thus we have produced a deterministic search strategy whose total travel
distance is never more than nine times the optimum.

\boxed{\text{The doubling out-and-back search described above is
deterministic and 9-competitive.}}

Confidence: 0.34
extracted_answer:
\text{The doubling out-and-back search described above is
deterministic and 9-competitive.}
